narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score and upper-middle family background. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 49.6% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold to reject your application—it's designed to prioritize candidates with the lowest predicted risk to ensure high bar passage rates. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not translate to bar success. Countering that strongly was your LSAT score of 37.5, slightly above the average of 36.7, which pulled the prediction meaningfully toward passing and showed your potential in standardized testing. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, a bit under the usual 3.2, also nudged things toward failure, though not as powerfully as your Year 3 deciles. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3 (versus an average around 5.7) added some additional concern about early inconsistencies in your grades. Being a full-time student, like most applicants at 92.7%, and your family income level helped a little by aligning with successful profiles, but they weren't decisive. Compared to typical students who get approved, your rankings in school deciles are noticeably lower, while your LSAT stands out positively—it's these gaps in academic deciles that tipped the scales most. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential as a Black woman leader in law; models like this reflect patterns in past data, but they don't capture your full story, resilience, or growth. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT or GPA-enhancing prep if needed, and reapplying with updated credentials—many students improve and succeed. We're rooting for you; law needs more voices like yours. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized guidance on strengthening your profile.
